1.3 Challenges in Multimodal Learning and Dataset Bias
Conceptually, multimodal learning leverages the fact that different data sources capture 
different aspects of disease, so combining them can improve diagnostic reliability relative to 
any single modality. Imaging reveals structural and anatomical patterns; biomarkers reflect 
molecular and systemic signals that may appear before any visi

1.4 Methodological Perspective and Research Focus
Although multimodal AI holds genuine promise for early detection, the literature includes 
studies whose reported gains may be inflated by unaddressed bias. Models may exploit scanner 
or protocol differences that correlate with class labels, producing strong metrics that do not 
necessarily reflect disease biology.
Fusion results can also be

Adding on this, (Debernardi 
et al., 2023) showed that these same biomarkers, when combined with plasma CA19-9 and 
patient age (PancRISK model), was able to detect PDAC up to two years before clinical 
diagnosis with an AUC of 0.89.97
2.3 Multi-Modal and Hybrid Approaches
(Dinesh et al., 2023) explored this direction by combining CNN-based imaging analysis with 
YOLO-driven biomarker detect

3.11 Alignment with Research Objectives
The methodology described in this chapter directly addresses the three research objectives 
established in,
Objective 1 (Bias-Aware Data Foundation) is addressed through systematic data acquisition 
and preprocessing for both CT and biomarker datasets, including leakage control and bias￾aware preparation steps. 
Objective 2 (Unimodal Model Development a

Adding on this, (Debernardi 
et al., 2023) showed that these same biomarkers, when combined with plasma CA19-9 and

2.3 Multi-Modal and Hybrid Approaches
(Dinesh et al., 2023) explored this direction by combining CNN-based imaging analysis with

3.8.4 Statistical Robustness
Statistical robustness was assessed primarily through repeated multi-seed analyses (including 
fusion sanity checks across seeds 0-4 and label-mismatch controls). Bootstrap confidence 
intervals were not part of the current notebook implementation; therefore, uncertainty is 
interpreted through variability across seeds and controls.

outcomes are important because they highlight the gap between benchmark accuracy numbers 
and the requirements of clinically deployable systems. They also reinforce why the bias-aware, 
transparency-first philosophy that guided this work was the right call.
6.2 Achievement of Research Objectives
Each of the three research objectives from Chapter 1 is revisited below.
6.2.1 Objective 1

For this project run, the processed CT cohort comprised 122 patients (40 cancer, 82 control). 
The preprocessing script indexed 24,627 slices and retained 23,319 slices after script-level 
quality control, with 1,308 slices dropped. These retained slices formed the baseline input to 
the notebook pipeline.
The core challenge is that the cancer and control datasets come from different instituti

Together, these objectives emphasize clinical relevance, methodological validity, and 
transparent reporting rather than metric-driven optimization alone. Detailed procedures used to 
carry out these objectives are provided in Chapter 3, with implementation specific details 
presented in Chapter 4.

How does dataset bias influence CT-based pancreatic cancer detection mod

at least 20 slices was selected.

4.4.1 CT Classification Model (ResNet50)
Architecture and Initialization: The CT classification model was built on ResNet50 (He et al., 
2016) with weights pretrained on ImageNet (IMAGENET1K_V2). The architecture comprises 
a 50-layer residual network organized into four convolutional stages with bottleneck blocks, 
followed by global average pooling that produces a 2048-dimensional embedd

60
5.3 CT Classification Model Results
The CT classification model was built on a ResNet50 backbone pretrained on ImageNet, with 
the final fully connected layer replaced to output two classes (cancer and control). The model 
was trained on the bias-mitigated, orientation-corrected, and body-segmented images (the 
ct_cropped dataset) using patient-level splitting with a stratified 70/30 train

101
7.2 Model Architecture 
The proposed model consists of two parallel deep learning branches, one for each modality:
• Imaging Pathway: A pretrained ResNet50 CNN will be used for feature extraction,
lower-level layers pretrained on ImageNet will be retained, while higher layers will be 
fine-tuned on the CT dataset. The output will be flattened and passed through a dense 
layer that result

the presence of systematic acquisition differences even after intensity standardization. Analysis 
with k = 6 identified finer substructure, including an outlier cancer cluster (cluster 0) containing 
images with distinct properties. These outlier images were removed during dataset filtering.

6.4 Contributions to Knowledge
The dissertation makes four main contributions:
Bias detection and mitigation pipeline for cross-dataset medical imaging: The study provides a 
documented, stepwise template for identifying and reducing scanner-specific intensity bias in 
cross-institutional CT studies. The pipeline includes quantitative bias detection using simple 
classifiers on low-level imag

Together, these objectives emphasize clinical relevance, methodological validity, and 
transparent reporting rather than metric-driven optimization alone. Detailed procedures used to 
carry out these objectives are provided in Chapter 3, with implementation specific details 
presented in Chapter 4.

3.7 Multimodal Fusion Methodology
3.7.1 The Synthetic Pairing Challenge
The most significant constraint on multimodal fusion in this research is the absence of paired 
patient data, no individual in the CT dataset has corresponding biomarker measurements and 
vice versa. Traditional multimodal learning assumes that each sample contains aligned 
observations from all modalities for the same su

The tendency to report only best case results, often from a single random seed or a single 
hyperparameter configuration, further inflates the apparent effectiveness of multimodal 
approaches. Publication bias compounds this problem: studies reporting null or negative fusion 
results are less likely to be published, creating a distorted literature in which multimodal fusion 
appears universall

3.11 Alignment with Research Objectives
The methodology described in this chapter directly addresses the three research objectives 
established in,
Objective 1 (Bias-Aware Data Foundation) is addressed through systematic data acquisition 
and preprocessing for both CT and biomarker datasets, including leakage control and bias￾aware preparation steps. 
Objective 2 (Unimodal Model Development a

3.7 Multimodal Fusion Methodology
3.7.1 The Synthetic Pairing Challenge
The most significant constraint on multimodal fusion in this research is the absence of paired 
patient data, no individual in the CT dataset has corresponding biomarker measurements and 
vice versa. Traditional multimodal learning assumes that each sample contains aligned 
observations from all modalities for the same su

All analyses use publicly available datasets. For CT, the processed cohort used in this project 
contains 122 patients (40 PDAC, 82 controls). Biomarker modelling uses the Debernardi et al. 
(2020) dataset with 590 samples and seven selected features, with data governance aligned to 
original usage terms.
One important limitation to flag upfront, there is no patient-level alignment between the

3.8.4 Statistical Robustness
Statistical robustness was assessed primarily through repeated multi-seed analyses (including 
fusion sanity checks across seeds 0-4 and label-mismatch controls). Bootstrap confidence 
intervals were not part of the current notebook implementation; therefore, uncertainty is 
interpreted through variability across seeds and controls.

results.question: What kinds of future work or next steps does the thesis suggest?
snippets:
Extending the framework to clinically realistic 
multi-class problems would require substantially larger and more diverse datasets.
Absence of prospective clinical validation: All experiments were conducted retrospectively on 
publicly available data. No prospective evaluation, radiologist comparison study, or clinical 
workflow integration was performed. Moving from retrospective computationa

Each of these extensions would increase the clinical utility of the 
framework but would require larger, more richly annotated datasets than are currently available 
in public repositories. The biomarker model’s three-class capability, demonstrated in the 
original (Debernardi et al., 2020) study, provides a natural starting point for this extension on 
the biomarker side.
6.6.4 Prospective V

While protocol uniformity 
requires institutional coordination and is a longer-term goal, its impact on the reliability of AI￾assisted diagnosis would be substantial. It would reduce the burden on computational methods 
required for reducing bias and enable more confident evaluation of model performance, because 
observed classification ability would be far less likely to reflect acquisition el

Together, these objectives emphasize clinical relevance, methodological validity, and 
transparent reporting rather than metric-driven optimization alone. Detailed procedures used to 
carry out these objectives are provided in Chapter 3, with implementation specific details 
presented in Chapter 4.
1.6 Research Questions
How does dataset bias influence CT-based pancreatic cancer detection mod

Residual domain bias in CT classification: The extreme standardization method dealt with 
pixel-level bias effectively, but it did not wipe out domain structure from deep feature 
representations. The CT model’s near-perfect performance therefore remains ambiguous: it 
could reflect genuine pathological feature learning, residual domain shortcut exploitation, or 
some mixture of both. Without

results.question: What is the main aim of this thesis?
snippets:
Aim and Objectives

Aim:

The goal of this research is to create a multi-modal deep learning diagnostic pipeline that utilises
contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) images in addition to urinary and blood
biomarkers to enhance accuracy and improve the early-stage detection of pancreatic cancer.
Detecting and mitigating this behavior is therefore a central focus of this dissertation.
Each of the three research objectives from Chapter 1 is revisited below.

This final chapter pulls together the main findings, assesses how well the three research
objectives were met, answers the five research questions from Chapter 1, and looks at what the
work contributes and where it falls short.
Final Thesis Report

results.question: What were the three main research objectives of the study?
snippets: Each of the three research objectives from Chapter 1 is revisited below.

The research aim, three specific
objectives, and five research questions were defined, centering on bias aware data preparation,
transparent unimodal evaluation, and rigorous assessment of fusion value under realistic
constraints.

This final chapter pulls together the main findings, assesses how well the three research
objectives were met, answers the five research questions from Chapter 1, and looks at what the
work contributes and where it falls short.

results.question: What seven urinary biomarker features were used for the biomarker model?
snippets:
5.4     Biomarker Classification Model Results

The biomarker classification model was implemented as a two-layer MLP (7 → 64 → 32 →
2) trained on seven urinary and blood-based features from the (Debernardi et al., 2020)
dataset.

Independent reproducibility evidence for urinary biomarker panel: The biomarker model’s
AUC of 0.944 provides independent computational evidence supporting the diagnostic utility
of the age, plasma CA19-9, creatinine, LYVE1, REG1B, TFF1, and REG1A feature panel for
PDAC detection.

A
multilayer perceptron trained on seven urinary biomarker features achieved an AUC of 0.944,
closely   reproducing   published   benchmarks.

Seven features were selected for the
classification task: age, plasma CA19-9, creatinine, LYVE1, REG1B, TFF1, and REG1A.

biomarker classifier used a seven-feature subset: age, plasma_CA19_9, creatinine, LYVE1,
REG1B, TFF1, and REG1A.

The model was trained using the AdamW optimizer with learning rate 1×10⁻³,
batch size 32, and 50 training epochs on the 60/20/20 train/validation/test split (354/118/118
samples).

Splitting was
stratified and performed in two steps: first, an 80/20 train-test split; second, a 25% split of the
train portion into validation, yielding an effective 60/20/20 train/validation/test partition.

Data splitting
was enforced strictly at the patient level: a stratified initial train versus temporary split (70/30)
was applied, and the temporary partition was then shuffled and divided into validation and test
subsets of equal size.

reproducibility of the urinary biomarker panel for PDAC detection.

AUC of 0.944, providing independent validation of the diagnostic utility of the urinary

of 0.944 against the published 0.936, confirming reproducibility with a different classifier

results.question: What did the thesis conclude about multimodal fusion under synthetic pairing?
snippets:
The bottom line from the fusion experiments is clear, under the synthetic pairing conditions and
residual domain bias present in this study, multimodal fusion does not add meaningful value
beyond what the stronger unimodal model already provides.

RQ3 asked whether multimodal fusion provides genuine complementary diagnostic value
beyond unimodal CT performance under synthetic pairing conditions.

Multimodal Fusion Contribution: The work implements and compares decision-level and
feature-level fusion under synthetic pairing constraints, with explicit controls for modality
dominance  and  cautious  interpretation  of  outcomes.

Second,
the study explicitly acknowledges the synthetic pairing limitation and interprets fusion results
accordingly, rather than presenting them as evidence of genuine multimodal synergy.

●   Multimodal fusion through both decision-level and feature-level strategies under
              synthetic pairing conditions.

results.question: What happened in the label-mismatch control when the CT weight was 0.3 versus 0.7?
snippets:
The real diagnostic value of the
experiments lay in the label-mismatch controls: at high CT weight (0.7), mismatch AUC
remained at 0.993, showing that the CT signal dominates regardless of whether the biomarker
labels are correct.

In implementation, fixed CT weights {0.3,
0.5, 0.7} were evaluated, and results were further stress-tested with multi-seed repeats and
label-mismatch controls.

At low CT weight (0.3), mismatch AUC collapsed to 0.352, confirming that
the biomarker component only contributes meaningfully when it receives correct labels and
sufficient influence in the weighting scheme.

Label-mismatch controls, in which
CT and biomarker samples were deliberately paired with the wrong labels, were conducted for
both decision-level and feature-level fusion.

CT Weight (wCT)     Label-Matched AUC     Label-Mismatch AUC
0.3     0.998     0.352
0.5     0.999     0.981
0.7     1.000     0.993

results.question: How was multimodal fusion implemented in the thesis?
snippets:
4.4.3 Multimodal Fusion Implementation

Multimodal fusion was implemented using two complementary strategies: decision-level fusion
operating  on  predicted  probabilities, and  feature-level  fusion  operating  on  intermediate
embeddings.

The multimodal fusion experiments aimed to demonstrate the methodological framework for
combining CT imaging and biomarker modalities, rather than to claim externally validated
clinical performance.

Multimodal fusion holds theoretical appeal but
requires careful validation, especially when modalities originate from different patient cohorts
and synthetic pairing must substitute for genuine patient-level correspondence.

The multimodal experiments rely on class-consistent synthetic pairing,
which is enough to test fusion methodology but cannot demonstrate true individual patient level
complementation.

This constraint shaped how the fusion results are interpreted throughout the
dissertation and is addressed directly in the discussion of future work.

results.question: What kinds of future work or next steps does the thesis suggest?
snippets:
Future
work should include reader comparison studies in which the model’s outputs are evaluated
alongside radiologist assessments on unseen cases, integration with clinical decision-support
workflows  to  assess  practical  utility  and  clinician  acceptance,  and  regulatory  pathway
engagement for any component that demonstrates robust prospective performance.

The findings and limitations point to several concrete directions for future work, starting with
the most pressing and moving toward longer-term goals.
6.6     Recommendations for Future Work

The next chapter pulls these findings together, assesses how far the research objectives have
been met, and sets out recommendations for future work.

This study has several limitations that constrain the interpretation of results and define
directions for future work.

results.answer_preview: To develop a bias-aware multimodal deep learning framework for pancreatic cancer detection using CT imaging and urinary biomarkers [Source 4].

results.answer_preview: 590 samples; split not stated in the evidence.

results.answer_preview: At CT weight 0.3, the label-mismatch AUC collapsed to 0.352, below chance; at 0.7, the mismatch AUC was perfect or near-perfect, remaining at 1.000 across the reported runs with at most a small drop in one seed [Source 1][Source 4].

results.question: How was multimodal fusion implemented in the thesis?

What is the main aim of this thesis?
results.answer_preview: To create a multi-modal deep learning diagnostic pipeline using CECT images plus urinary and blood biomarkers to improve accuracy and early-stage detection of pancreatic cancer [Source 1].

results.question: What were the three main research objectives of the study?
How many CT patients were used in the processed cohort and how were they split?
What was the baseline bias AUC and what did extreme standardization reduce it to?
results.answer_preview: Baseline bias AUC was 0.866, and extreme standardization reduced it to 0.569 [Source 4].

results.question: What CT model architecture was used in the thesis?
What seven urinary biomarker features were used for the biomarker model?
results.answer_preview: age, plasma CA19-9, creatinine, LYVE1, REG1B, TFF1, and REG1A [Source 2]

results.question: How many biomarker samples were used and what was the train validation test split?
What AUC did the biomarker MLP achieve and how did it compare with the original study?
results.answer_preview: The biomarker MLP achieved an AUC of 0.944, compared with the original study’s 0.936 [Source 1].

results.question: What did the thesis conclude about multimodal fusion under synthetic pairing?
results.answer_preview: It concluded that multimodal fusion did not add meaningful value beyond the stronger unimodal model under synthetic pairing conditions [Source 1].

results.question: What happened in the label-mismatch control when the CT weight was 0.3 versus 0.7?
results.answer_preview: At 0.3, mismatch AUC fell to 0.352; at 0.7, it stayed high at 0.993 [Source 3][Source 1].

results.question: How was multimodal fusion implemented in the thesis?
results.answer_preview: It was implemented with two complementary strategies: decision-level fusion on predicted probabilities and feature-level fusion on intermediate embeddings [Source 1].

results.question: What kinds of future work or next steps does the thesis suggest?
results.answer_preview: Reader comparison studies against radiologist assessments, integration into clinical decision-support workflows, and regulatory pathway engagement for components with robust prospective performance [Source 1].
